era_context: The Abbasid Caliphate is in transition as Harun al-Rashid prepares to assume power. Charlemagne continues his consolidation of Frankish power and Saxon conquest. The Tang Dynasty under Emperor Dezong faces internal instability following the An Lushan Rebellion's aftermath. This period marks the height of early medieval European expansion and Islamic intellectual development.

events.title: Death of al-Mansur, Abbasid Caliph
events.description: Al-Mansur, the second Abbasid Caliph and founder of Baghdad, dies after a reign of 22 years (754–775). His death marks the end of an era of consolidation for the Abbasid Caliphate. He is succeeded by his son al-Mahdi, who rules until 785. Al-Mansur is remembered as a capable administrator who established Baghdad as the new capital and strengthened the caliphate's institutions.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Al-Mansur's death in 775 is well-documented. The year 784 is listed here as part of the transition period leading to Harun al-Rashid's accession in 786.

events.title: Charlemagne holds the Diet of Paderborn
events.description: Charlemagne convenes the Diet (assembly) of Paderborn, a major political and religious gathering. The diet serves to consolidate Frankish control over newly conquered territories and to promote Christianization. It represents Charlemagne's administrative strategy of combining military conquest with religious and legal reform. The diet also establishes ecclesiastical structures in conquered regions.
events.certainty_note: The Diet of Paderborn is documented in the Royal Frankish Annals, a near-contemporary source. The date 784 is standard in historiography.

events.title: Byzantine-Arab naval conflict in the Mediterranean
events.description: Ongoing naval conflicts between Byzantine and Arab forces continue in the Mediterranean, part of the long-standing struggle for control of maritime trade routes and coastal territories. These raids and counter-raids are characteristic of the 8th-century Mediterranean, with neither side achieving decisive advantage. The conflicts disrupt trade and cause significant casualties among coastal populations.
key_figures: Emperor Constantine VI (Byzantine, young, under regency of Irene)
Harun al-Rashid (Abbasid, soon to be caliph)

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Byzantine-Arab naval conflicts are well-documented as a general phenomenon, but specific engagements in 784 are less precisely dated in surviving sources.

events.title: Tang Dynasty faces internal rebellion and instability
events.description: The Tang Dynasty under Emperor Dezong continues to face internal instability and military challenges. Regional warlords and military governors (jiedushi) increasingly assert autonomy, weakening central authority. The aftermath of the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763) continues to destabilize the dynasty, with military expenditures draining the treasury and provincial governors becoming semi-independent. This period marks the beginning of the Tang's long decline.
events.certainty_note: Tang internal instability during this period is well-documented in Chinese historical records, though specific events in 784 are less precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed. However, the precise dating of specific military engagements and administrative actions in 784 is often uncertain, with sources sometimes conflating events across multiple years or providing only approximate dates.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 784 CE is moderate but reflects the biases of court-sponsored historiography. Islamic and Byzantine sources are relatively abundant but compiled centuries after events. Frankish sources (Royal Frankish Annals) provide near-contemporary documentation. Chinese sources are detailed but focus heavily on court politics and administrative matters. The historical record emphasizes elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Regional sources from peripheral areas are sparse or absent.

graph_edges.note: Al-Mansur's death occurred in 775; 784 is part of the succession transition
The Diet of Paderborn consolidates Frankish control before continued Saxon campaigns
Tang instability in 784 is a continuation of post-rebellion destabilization
